nativeRange.habitat: Rao's Dart Goby inhabits shallow coastal waters across its range, including mangrove swamps, estuaries, inlet channels, and lagoons over mud, sand, or rock substrates. In the wild the species tends to form loose surface aggregations.

diet.notes: Generally accepts frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and other meaty marine foods. Has been reported to accept enriched live brine shrimp and glass shrimp. Color may fade if not offered a sufficiently varied and nutritious diet; target feeding twice daily is generally recommended.

imageCandidates.notes: Julien Renoult; 2048x1536. Only commercial-friendly photo found. In-situ estuary shot showing several live individuals shoaling among oyster shells; slender bodies with dark lateral stripe visible. Caveat: subjects small in a busy natural background rather than a striking close-up.

summary: Rao's Dart Goby is a small, peaceful schooling species from the Indo-West Pacific that tends to hover in loose aggregations near the water's surface. Generally considered reef-safe, it is well suited to nano and small reef aquariums provided the tank has a tightly sealed lid.
careNotes:
Rao's Dart Goby, also sold as Rao's Hover Goby, is a small species generally reaching around 3–4 cm, making it well-suited for smaller reef setups. It is prone to jumping, so a tightly fitting aquarium lid is strongly advised. Groups or pairs tend to settle in more readily than single specimens, and introducing individuals simultaneously into an established tank has been reported to improve acclimation. A well-established aquarium with abundant live rock, caves, and crevices is generally recommended to provide shelter and supplemental foraging opportunities. Diet quality matters: coloration and overall condition have been noted to deteriorate without a sufficiently varied, nutrition-rich menu of meaty foods.

In the wild this species inhabits both fully marine and brackish environments — mangroves and estuaries as well as open coastal shallows — though aquarium care targets standard reef salinity.
breedingNotes: The species reproduces via external fertilization with batch spawning, and males are reported to exhibit nest-guarding behavior. Captive breeding has not been widely documented in the hobby literature.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Specific species product page; lists care level as Easy, min tank 10 gal, temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirms scientific name, family (Microdesmidae), order (Gobiiformes), max size 3.5 cm SL, wild temperature range 26.4–29.3°C (modeled), Indo-West Pacific distribution, habitat in mangroves and coastal shallows. Temperature disagreement with hobby sources: FishBase wild range is 26.4–29.3°C vs hobby 22.2–25.6°C (72–78°F); averaged to 24.0–27.5°C.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Lists care level as Moderate (vs LiveAquaria Easy); confirms same water parameters and reef-safe status. Care level averaged to intermediate. Notes jump risk and need for tight-fitting lid. Emphasizes enriched/live foods for diet quality.

fish.breedingNotes: The species reproduces via external fertilization with batch spawning, and males are reported to exhibit nest-guarding behavior. Captive breeding is not widely documented.
